medications.drug_name: gentle active rewarming (warm — not hot — water) and warmth/gloves
medications.indication_rationale: Gentle rewarming and warmth abort the attack and restore perfusion; avoid very hot water/heating pads on numb fingers (burn risk).

medications.drug_name: NSAID (e.g. meloxicam) / simple analgesia for arthralgia
medications.indication_rationale: A short NSAID course (with usual cautions) or paracetamol can help the symmetric arthralgia while arranging review.
medications.dose_route_frequency: within recommended limits; GP to confirm

medications.indication_rationale: A CCB is first-line for severe secondary Raynaud's but should be GP/specialist-initiated after review, not started over chat.
No acute evidence base for controlling the flare.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Hand aids, warming strategies and self-care support while hands are painful.
Support with heating/meals for an isolated patient in a cold home.

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Cold avoidance, warm gloves (heated gloves), warm home
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Cold avoidance is a cornerstone of Raynaud's management.
No evidence for controlling the flare.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Watch for any single finger staying white/numb for over 2 hours despite warming, a finger tip turning black, or one limb becoming persistently cold/pale, and go to ED if they occur; rewarm gently.
Warm the home, wear thermal/heated gloves indoors, avoid cold exposure, and arrange help with food/medication while hands are painful.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces Raynaud's triggers and maintains self-care.

patient_education_points.point: Cold has set off a Raynaud's attack — your finger blood vessels clamp shut (white), starve of oxygen (blue), then flood back (red and painful). This fits your UCTD, and your joints are also flaring.
Rewarm your hands gently in warm — not hot — water (numb fingers can burn), warm your home, and wear gloves; the colour should return over the next hour.

Because both hands are recovering with warming and no finger is black, this isn't a blocked artery — but arrange a GP/rheumatology review for a blood-vessel medicine (calcium-channel blocker) and to review your treatment.

Go to ED if any single finger stays completely white and numb for more than 2 hours despite warming, a finger tip turns dark blue/black, or one hand/limb becomes persistently cold, pale and pulseless.

follow_up_plan.interval: In-person GP/rheumatology review within a week (sooner if worsening) for CCB/DMARD and monitoring
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Digital viability/perfusion, synovitis, CTD progression (serology/capillaroscopy), medication plan, home warming/self-care
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: A persistently white/numb or black digit, or a cold pulseless limb — go to ED.

must_include_items: Screen and safety-net critical digital ischaemia (persistent white/numb or black digit) and arterial embolism (cold pulseless limb)
Advise gentle rewarming (warm not hot), cold avoidance and gloves; treat arthralgia with simple analgesia/NSAID
Arrange GP/rheumatology review (calcium-channel blocker, DMARD, CTD monitoring)
Address the cold home and loss of hand function
errors_of_commission: Reflexive ambulance for a recovering Raynaud's attack
Advising very hot water/heating pads on numb fingers
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires (1) critical-ischaemia + embolism safety-netting, (2) safe rewarming/cold-avoidance advice, and (3) a rheumatology review pathway. Missing critical ischaemia OR reflexively over-escalating a recovering attack are the failure modes.
